data.title: Macy's declares $0.1824 dividend
data.description:

  • Forward yield 5.94%

  • See M Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Boeing in tentative deal to avoid guilty plea, prosecution in fatal crashes case - Reuters
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) has reached a tentative non-prosecution agreement with U.S. prosecutors in a fraud case stemming from two fatal 737 MAX plane crashes that killed 346 people, Reuters reported Friday.

The agreement would forestall a June 23 trial date the company faces on a charge it misled U.S. regulators about a crucial flight control system on the 737 MAX, according to the report.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) has no longer agreed to plead guilty in the case, prosecutors reportedly told family members of crash victims during a Friday meeting, marking a change in the company's posture after a judge rejected a previous plea agreement in December.

U.S. Department of Justice officials are still considering whether to proceed with a non-prosecution agreement or take Boeing (BA) to trial, a DoJ official reportedly told family memebers at the meeting.

No final decision has been made, and Boeing (BA) and DoJ officials have not yet exchanged papers to negotiate final details of any nonprosecution agreement, the official reportedly said.

data.title: SMCI most shorted S&P 500 technology stock in April; Microsoft least shorted
data.description:
Short sellers increased their bets against the information technology sector in April. The sector which holds the highest weightage on the S&P had an average short interest of 1.78% in April, up from 1.74% at the end of March. 

The S&P 500’s information technology sector (NYSEARCA:XLK) has grown 0.83% so far this year, compared to a 0.60% rise in the broader benchmark index. 

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) remained the most shorted stock in the sector in April, while Microsoft (MSFT) took the spot as the least shorted stock.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Enphase Energy (ENPH), and Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) were the top three most shorted stocks in April. Short interest in SMCI declined to 21.15% in April, from 22.84% at the end of March. 

While, Enphase Energy (ENPH) and Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) had a short interest of 13.34%, and 12.07% respectively. 

Microsoft (MSFT) was the least shorted stock in the sector last month, with a short interest of 0.69%, preceded by Apple (AAPL) and Teledyne Technologies (TDY), with short interests of 0.73% and 1.03% respectively. 

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

Electronic Manufacturing Services was the most shorted industry within the information technology sector, with 3.79% short interest in April, up from 3.36% in March.  

IT Services was the second most shorted industry with 2.32% short interest as of April 30, compared to 2.31% at the end of March. 

Electronic Equipment, Instruments & Components was the least shorted industry in the sector this month as well, with a short interest of 1.44%.

data.title: Neurogene downgraded to Neutral at Baird on regulatory uncertainty
data.description:
• Neurogene (NASDAQ:NGNE) was downgraded to Neutral from Outperform at Baird, citing increasing regulatory uncertainty and a higher approval bar at the FDA under new leadership.
  • The analysts at Baird said that despite the stock rebound since April, they believe the FDA's bar for approval is indeed moving higher, as former head of Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, Peter Marks, has been replaced by a far more data-driven Vinay Prasad. 
  • Refering to Taysha Gene Therapies's (TSHA) recent data, the analysts said, "The data to date for the Rett syndrome gene therapies from NGNE and TSHA seem messy to us, and closer to Elevidys than a 'parachute'. 
  • Baird analysts have also cut price target for the company to $24 from previous $38.
  • The stock is down 11.7% so far today.

data.title: U.S. stocks are muted after softer inflation data slows enthusiasm
data.description:
Wall Street major averages were little changed in Friday's late afternoon trade as investors received the latest consume sentiment report, while the S&P 500 hoped to extend its four-day straight rally.

Both the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Dow (DJI) were 0.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was flat.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was last flat at 4.44%, and the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.99%. 

Wall Street on Thursday ended mixed, but its benchmark S&P 500 index extended a rally to four straight days and further solidified itself in positive territory for the year.

“Markets continued to advance over the last 24 hours, as weak U.S. inflation data and lower oil prices raised expectations that the Federal Reserve would still cut rates this year,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

A key factor driving the rally was the U.S. PPI data for April, which showed inflation pressures were much softer than expected.

“Thursday's softer-than-expected PPI inflation print helps to confirm that inflation is moving in the right direction, especially after Tuesday's soft CPI report,” said Clark Bellin, president and chief investment officer at Bellwether Wealth. “It's encouraging that the inflation data has softened in April, especially as we brace for the potential for higher inflation over the coming months as tariffs work their way through the economy.”

In addition, U.S. import and export prices surprised to the upside in April, housing starts rose in April, and building permits dropped more than expected.

On the economic side, the University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index edged down to 50.8 in May (preliminary) versus the 53.1 consensus level.

data.title: Occidental, UAE's ADNOC to explore JV to develop South Texas direct air capture hub
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) and its 1PointFive low-carbon subsidiary said Friday they reached an agreement with ADNOC to evaluate a potential joint venture to develop a direct air capture facility in South Texas.

As part of the joint venture, ADNOC will consider investing au much as $500M for the development of a DAC facility designed to capture 500K metric tons/year of carbon dioxide.

Occidental (NYSE:OXY) and ADNOC have been discussing opportunities to collaborate on carbon capture, utilization and storage projects in the U.S. and United Arab Emirates since signing a memorandum of understanding in 2023.

The announcement follows several significant milestones in the development of DAC, including Occidental's (OXY) progress on STRATOS, its first DAC facility in West Texas, which is on track to start commercial operations in 2025, and a U.S. Department of Energy award for up to $650M to support development of the South Texas DAC Hub.

data.title: Sunstone's renovations to pay off, earning upgrade at Evercore to Outperform
data.description:
Construction delays at its Miami property might have caused Sunstone (NYSE:SHO) to miss the peak Q1 season, but “meaningful” tailwinds from its major renovation projects and earnings growth that should outpace its hotel REIT peers gives Evercore ISI analyst Duane Pfennigwerth reason to be more encouraged on the company’s potential, giving the stock an upgrade Outperform from In-Line.    

Last quarter, Sunstone (NYSE:SHO) saw a 2% year-over-year improvement in RevPAR, driven by strong results in its New Orleans and Washington D.C. properties, and tailwinds from the Marriott Long Beach renovation. This contributed to a 5% improvement in Q1 EBITDA.

But as trends slowed later in the quarter, Sunstone (NYSE:SHO) took the step to lower expectations for Q2 and Q3, taking into account continued weakness in government travel, little to no improvement in the international demand imbalance, and more subdued demand at its Wailea, Hawaii property.  

Despite the more conservative outlook from the company, Pfennigwerth sees growth for Sunstone (SHO) beginning in the second half of this year on renovation tailwinds, while a successful wine country asset sale would further improve Sunstone’s valuation.

Meanwhile, Pfennigwerth downgraded Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) to In-Line from Outperform, citing anticipated renovation headwinds at its Miami property and the company’s reliance on asset sales to support capital returns and meet capex goals.

Given that Park (PK) has demonstrated its ability to successfully reposition assets, like Orlando Bonnet Creek, and as visibility into the completion in Miami improves, Pfennigwerth says he “may look to revisit” Park’s neutral rating.

Despite a three-day losing streak this week, Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) is set to close higher for a sixth straight week.

data.title: Flowers Foods signals longer timeline for category recovery as premiumization strategy advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flowers Foods (FLO) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ryals McMullian stated, "While none of us here are satisfied with our absolute performance in the quarter, we did hold unit share in a category that faced greater than expected declines. Those results in an uncertain economic environment do highlight the importance of our portfolio strategy and the strength of our brands."
  • McMullian said the company is investing in innovation and "targeting significant opportunities in faster growing categories and adjacencies... By aligning our portfolio with evolving consumer taste and targeting new white space for growth, we aim to maximize near-term performance while developing our brands and capabilities to drive sustainable growth over the long-term."
  • McMullian indicated ongoing confidence in the company’s initiatives to enhance shareholder value and meet long-term financial targets.
  • CFO Steve Kinsey explained, "When you look at kind of what's impacting that, obviously, we said category trends are a big consideration when we pull together our guidance change. So, obviously, that's going to impact the gross margin line, tariffs obviously impact the input costs. So, that's primarily gross margin."

Outlook

  • McMullian commented that recovery in the core packaged bread category is unlikely this year, saying, "Given the trends that we saw in the first quarter where things actually weakened further than we had anticipated, my mind is already moving to '26. So, I don't know that we see a tremendous amount of improvement this year."
  • The company expects new business wins and spring reset space gains to help mitigate headwinds, but improvement is expected "when consumer health returns and it eventually will."
  • Innovation and brand differentiation remain central to Flowers Foods' approach for reinvigorating traditional bread segments and addressing health-oriented consumer trends.

Financial Results

  • Management did not disclose explicit revenue, profit, or EPS figures for Q1 2025 during the call.
  • Kinsey noted the impact of tariffs and category weakness on gross margin, with cost saving efforts in SD&A intended to "mitigate some of the impact of the top line challenge as well as the tariffs."
  • The company is continuing supply chain optimization and recently closed an older fresh bread, bun, and roll plant as part of this program.

Q&A

  • Jim Salera, Stephens: Asked about unit share stabilization in core packaged bread and whether improvement could occur this year. McMullian emphasized uncertainty, citing economic and consumer health factors, and suggested improvement may not occur until 2026.
  • Salera followed up on Wonder cake innovation. McMullian responded that it is too early to determine whether growth is incremental or a swap, but current performance has offset legacy cake business declines.
  • Max Gumport, BNP Paribas: Inquired about promotional stance and consumer behavior. McMullian said, "Most of that increase in promotional activity was around our more differentiated offerings like Dave's Killer Bread... We've never been the most highly promoted a player...we primarily use it to drive trial."
  • Gumport also queried about the structural shift to healthier eating. McMullian highlighted ongoing innovation under DKB, Canyon Bakehouse, Keto, and the Simple Mills acquisition to address health and wellness trends.
  • Mitchell Pinheiro, Sturdivant & Company: Questioned the EBITDA margin guidance reduction and distribution between gross margin and SD&A. Kinsey attributed most of the impact to gross margin due to tariffs and category challenges, with cost savings in SD&A to offset some pressure.
  • Steve Powers, Deutsche Bank: Sought clarity on DKB snacking and Simple Mills performance and tariff impacts. McMullian confirmed both are performing well, with caution factored into outlooks. Kinsey detailed the shift in tariff exposure from Canada/Mexico to other countries and its modeled impact.
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: Asked about private label and away-from-home business weakness. McMullian said foodservice sales remain weak, but margins have improved due to restructuring, and new private label business is expected to replenish volume.
  • Marks also asked about smaller loaf SKUs. McMullian said new SKUs have been added with good early returns, targeting smaller households and value-oriented consumers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern over the timing of category recovery, the efficacy of innovation, and the ability to manage margin pressures. The Q&A tone was neutral to slightly negative, reflecting skepticism about near-term improvement.
  • Management maintained a measured and realistic tone, acknowledging disappointment in quarterly performance but expressing confidence in strategic initiatives and innovation, using forward-looking language such as "we aim" and "I remain confident."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained cautious, but there was a shift toward accepting a longer recovery timeline and more explicit acknowledgment of category headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism to a more conservative stance, with McMullian now looking to 2026 for meaningful improvement rather than the second half of 2025.
  • The focus on innovation and premiumization intensified, as did references to structural shifts in consumer preferences and the impact of tariffs.
  • Analyst questions pivoted more toward how and when the company will address the challenges in traditional bread categories, and the expected timeline for category stabilization was pushed out compared to last quarter.
  • Both quarters featured management confidence in brand strength and innovation, but the current quarter included more explicit discussion of mitigation strategies and the impact of external headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly cited greater-than-expected category declines, continued economic uncertainty, consumer health, and evolving tariffs as key risks.
  • Tariffs on ingredients from outside the US, including China and other countries, are expected to pressure gross margins, with mitigation strategies focused on cost savings and supply chain optimization.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the potential for structural category weakness, the effectiveness of promotional and innovation strategies, and the risk that premiumization may not offset volume declines in traditional segments.

Final Takeaway

Flowers Foods' leadership acknowledged ongoing category declines and economic uncertainty, reinforcing a strategy focused on innovation, targeted promotions, and premiumization. The company does not foresee substantial improvement in the core bread category until at least 2026, but believes its evolving portfolio, cost-saving initiatives, and targeted investments position it to capture growth opportunities when consumer health rebounds. Management maintains confidence in its ability to drive long-term shareholder value despite near-term headwinds and a delayed recovery outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intel disputes $421M fine from EU regulators stemming from 16-year-old case: report
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) attorneys disputed the magnitude of a $421.4M fine levied against the semiconductor company by the European Commission for allegedly blocking rival firms from the market, according to Reuters.

The case dates back to 2009, when the Commission hit Intel with a $1.18B fine for preventing fair competition by Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), according to the report. Intel appealed the case to the General Court, and successfully reduced the scope of the fine in 2022. However, judges still found Intel culpable of making payments to HP (HPE), Acer and Lenovo to stall or block them from using products made by competitors during a period ranging from 2002 to 2006.

"The Commission cannot sustain a finding that there was an overall strategy to foreclose competitors from the entire x86 chips market," Intel lawyer Daniel Beard said to judges on Friday, according to the report. 

"The naked restrictions can't be treated as in effect of equal weight to each of the pricing practices which were overturned," Beard added. "Nor do they have the same sort of cumulative effect or strategic weight."

A ruling on the final size of the fine is expected sometime in the next few months, the report said.

data.title: FTX to pay $5B to creditors as part of defunct crypto exchange's recovery plan
data.description:
FTX (FTT-USD), the now-defunct crypto exchange founded by convicted felon Sam Bankman-Fried, is returning more than $5B to creditors starting May 30, as part of its broader recovery plan to manage assets for creditor repayments.

Holders of allowed claims in the reorganization plan's convenience and non-convenience classes that have completed the pre-distribution requirements should expect to receive funds from their selected distribution service provider -- either custodians Bitgo or Kraken -- within one to three from May 30, the FTX estate said.

The company's second distribution has been structured as: allowed Class 5A dotcom customer entitlement claims will receive a 72% distribution; allowed Class 5B U.S. customer entitlement claims will receive a 54% distribution; allowed Classes 6A general unsecured claims and 6B digital asset loan claims will each receive a 61% distribution; and allowed Class 7 convenience claims will receive a 120% distribution.

"The scope and magnitude of the FTX creditor base makes this an unprecedented distribution process, and today's announcement reflects the outstanding success of the recovery and coordination efforts of our team of professionals," FTX CEO John J. Ray III said in a statement. "Our focus remains on recovering more for creditors and resolving outstanding claims."

Last August, U.S. District Judge Peter Castel ordered the defunct exchange and its trading partner, Alameda Research, to pay $12.7B to their creditors, a move that ended a 20-month lawsuit filed by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission.
